issue_title: ipdb shows modified file instead of file from memory
issue_body:
I typically run a file through `ipdb` and sometimes while running it through the debbuger, I figure out the bugs and incorporate it into the code.

Once that is done, I would still expect that ipdb would show the file in memory, rather the file on disk(if it is modified). However, it shows the modified file but actually runs the file already loaded into memory.

Is this something that can be fixed?

issue_title: Unable to use external lua libraries with lupa-1.5 wheels
issue_body:
My project currently depends on some external lua libraries that I am installing with luarocks.
However, since lupa-1.5 now provides wheels on pypi build with the bundled lua-5.3 code I am unable to install external libraries (since the lua runtime is static linked into the lupa library)

Is there some way around this or do I have to somehow avoid using the lupa wheel?
As far as I'm aware there's no way to specify wheel vs source build in a requirements.txt so this new duality of bundled vs system lua makes using lupa in a project with non-trivial dependencies much more complex.

issue_title: PyOWM 2.5 / Python 3.5: registry calls return "None"
issue_body:
Following the example at https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/wiki/Usage-examples#retrieving-city-id-for-a-location
```
    registry = owm.city_id_registry()
    print(registry.location_for("Gillingham"))
    print(registry.id_for("Gillingham"))
    print(registry.id_for("London,GB"))
```
I get ```None``` in all cases, whether I put UK, GB or just nothing after the town.

So I followed the docs at https://pyowm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyowm.webapi25.html#module-pyowm.webapi25.cityidregistry and read the source at https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/webapi25/cityidregistry.py and put

```    registry = owm.city_id_registry('/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pyowm/webapi25/cityids/%02d-%02d.txt')```

and got ```TypeError: city_id_registry() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given```

The town definitely exists:
```
/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/pyowm/webapi25/cityids/103-108.txt
3106:Gillingham,2648657,51.389141,0.54863,GB
3107:Gillingham,2648658,51.03833,-2.27611,GB
```

I also found a reference to the registry not working back in January, here:
https://tasteandseejesus.com/2016/01/13/3-hour-forecasts-with-openweathermap-and-python/

issue_title: can you give a quick start?
diff fails on non-UTF8 encodings
issue_body:
In common.py, method `diff(self, old, new, rel_path=None)` has a bug in line 457:
`  for non_empty_diff in filter(None, diff_result.decode('utf8').split('Index: ')):`
Here silently UTF-8 encoding is assumed. But this fails for non UTF-8 files (e.g. for files with Latin-1 (= ISO 8859-1) encoding):

issue_title: Please document that this is a wrapper for the CLI svn client
issue_body: This library just wraps the `svn` commandline client, executing it using `subprocess`. In my opinion, this should be documented in the README, as it has implications for performance (each command needs to execute `svn`, which has to re-establish a HTTPS connection, etc.) as well as deployment (the machine the code runs on needs the `svn` CLI client).

issue_title: Support for WaveNet
Update metadata
issue_body:
Just noticed there's a `create` method and a `get_metadata` method using the same endpoint, but no `update` method. Here's some relevant info from Socrata on the `/api/views.json` endpoint:

> `PUT` to `/api/views/unique_id.json` with a subset of what you want to update.  For instance, if you want to add tags to the previously created dataset (currently this is the only way to add tags), you can simply `PUT` to the endpoint with:
```json
{
  "tags": ["these", "words", "are", "tags"]
}
```
> You'll probably also want to make this dataset public and publish it, so you'll want to perform the two following operations as well:
`PUT /views/unique_id?method=setPermission&value=public.read`
`POST /api/views/unique_id/publication.json`

> One important thing to note about updates is that if you want to update something within the "custom metadata", you will have to copy the entire metadata object since it does a replace.

Note that I've heard over the year(s) that the views endpoint is/will be deprecated, but until there's a clear alternative it probably makes sense to continue to use it.

Anyway, I may get a chance to work on this, but wanted to post the details here in case anyone else is interested.

issue_title: [Question] zip_safe setting if Wheels
issue_body:
### 1. Briefly

I'm not sure, is the parameter `zip_safe` needs, **if** developer build package use [**Wheels**](https://pythonwheels.com/).

### 2. Steps to reproduce

I create my Python package → [**I build my package, use Wheels**](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#platform-wheels):

    python setup.py bdist_wheel

I publish my package to PyPI use [**Twine**](https://github.com/pypa/twine):

    twine upload dist/*

I install my package use pip:

    pip install <my_package>

### 3. Essence of the question

**If** I build my package, use `python setup.py sdist`, `zip_safe` parameter have a meaning:

**If** `zip_safe = True`:

    package add to my `site-packages` directory, packed to `.egg` format;

**else**:

    package add to `site-packages` directory unpacked.

**But if** I build my package, use `python setup.py sdist`, I get same behavior **for** `zip_safe` `true` **and** `false` **or if** I don't have `zip_safe` parameter. Package add to `site-packages` directory unpacked.

### 4. pyroma

**If** I have line `zip_safe = True` or `zip_safe = False`:

```

vis_data = gensimvis.prepare(lda, corpus, dictionary)
```

Minimal code to duplicate is below. I'm using Python 3.4.3 on a Mac (via Anaconda), gensim 0.10.3, and pyLDAvis 1.1.0. Hope I'm just doing something obviously dumb. Thanks for any help you can provide.

```
import gensim
from gensim import corpora, models, similarities

from collections import defaultdict
```

so that the discussion can be followed by others.

Firstly, thank-you very much for writing behaving - it's saved me huge amounts of my time.

Secondly, I appreciate the way that you used Python namespace packages for your components,
and I would like to propose that as the way forward for the behave community to package up step libraries in a way for making them work together. And I like the idea of the behaving namespace being completely independent from the behave base.

issue_title: ZFS filesystem support
issue_body:
I've made a simple modification of _pslinux.py based on master branch downloaded at Sep 21 19:27 UTC. 

The bellow modification allow users to list ZFS filesystems on Linux (http://zfsonlinux.org/) through psutil.disk_partitions()

```
# diff _pslinux.py-original _pslinux.py
666,667c666,669
<             if not line.startswith("nodev"):
<                 phydevs.append(line.strip())

---
>             if line.startswith("nodev") and line.find('zfs') == -1:
>                 continue
>             else:
>                 phydevs.append(line.replace('nodev','').strip())
```

I've made tests and it is working fine.

This is the result:

```
>>> import psutil
>>> psutil.disk_partitions()
[sdiskpart(device='/dev/xvda1', mountpoint='/', fstype='ext4', opts='rw'), sdiskpart(device='datastore_var/var', mountpoint='/var', fstype='zfs', opts='rw'), sdiskpart(device='datastore_backup/backup', mountpoint='/backup', fstype='zfs', opts='rw,noatime,xattr')]
```

This is my contribution with this nice project. I hope you include it in future versions.

:)

issue_title: Move Oauth refresh token out of the config file
issue_body:
Currently the oauth token is stored in the rtv config file as
```
[oauth]
acccess_token=...
refresh_token=...
```
I was thinking that instead of doing this, we could keep a separate key file in the same directory. This would be cleaner in situations where the user doesn't have a config file, or their config file is read-only. Something like

**rtv.oauth** or **oauth.txt**
```
oauth_refresh_token=refresh_token.
```

Could even get fancy and store tokens for multiple users in the same file, e.g.
```
user_1=refresh_token...
user_2=refresh_token...
user_3=refresh_token...
```
idk about that part though..

I looked what PRAW does, they have do have a setting in their main config for the *oauth_refresh_token*. However I think ours is a bit different because we're programatically overwriting the values. http://praw.readthedocs.org/en/latest/pages/configuration_files.html#configuration-files

```
    # Wait for button press

    GPIO.output(7, GPIO.LOW)
    term.println("Button pressed")

    # Wait for button release
```

issue_title: sorter-false for bootstrap themes
Requests objects should be passed to the refresh token handlers
issue_body:
To store and retrieve refresh tokens I use the database connection which is stored in the `app` object. Therefore `retrieve_refresh_token` and `store_refresh_token` should at least receive the `request` object as an argument, so that I can access the `request.app` attribute. Otherwise I am forced to put all the handlers inside `app.py`, which will quickly become bloated and unreadable.

Is there a temporary solution while this is pondered and hopefully included?

issue_title: visualize_saliency breaks down if input_shape is (None, None, 3)
issue_body:
My network accepts images of variable height and width, which can be represented by `x = Input(1, None, None, 3)`. Invoking the `vis.visualization.visualize_saliency` on this network will throw the following error:

```shell
ValueError: Cannot feed value of shape (1, 3, 235, 224) for Tensor 'input_1_2:0', which has shape '(?, ?, ?, 3)'
```

I believe the problem is in `Optimizer._get_seed_input(seed_input)` (optimizer.py L95, on master), where an assumption incorrectly swaps the channel dim from its correct position to an incorrect one:
```py
# Only possible if channel idx is out of place.
if seed_input.shape != desired_shape:
    seed_input = np.moveaxis(seed_input, -1, 1)
return seed_input.astype(K.floatx())
```

For example, let's say I'm feeding an image of `(423, 451, 3)`. `seed_input.shape == (1, 423, 451, 3) != (1, None, None, 3) == desired_shape` will pass and `seed_input.shape` will become `(1, 3, 451, 423)`.

This can be corrected by removing this condition and assuming the user knows what to input. However, if this condition must be kept, then maybe we could make this checking more strict. Something in the lines of:
```py
channels = 1 if K.image_data_format() == 'channels_first' else -1
if seed_input.shape[channels] != desired_shape[channels]:
    seed_input = np.moveaxis(seed_input, -1, 1)
```

- [x] Check that you are up-to-date with the master branch of keras-vis. You can update with:
pip install git+git://github.com/raghakot/keras-vis.git --upgrade --no-deps
- [x] If running on TensorFlow, check that you are up-to-date with the latest version. The installation instructions can be found [here](https://www.tensorflow.org/get_started/os_setup).

issue_title: How can dump all hash160, and address with unspent balances?
Code Versioning for `fpy_auxiliary`
issue_body:
Currently, when a unit test requires the `autoclass` feature, `fortpy` compiles the auxiliary module automatically, but only if the code version has changed. But what defines the code version? The template version number shipping with `fortpy` will hardly ever change, but when it does, then the code knows to recompile. However, when the user's version changes, we have no idea so the newest versions of the auxiliary module don't necessarily get copied over.

We could have it recompile and copy auxiliary every time a test is run to be safe, but that is really wasteful because most user types don't change very often in a code's development cycle. We could have the user set the version number in a separate file, but that can be irritating for them. Maybe it is best to have a flag in `runtests.py` that forces a recompile and copy. Then, if the user is having issues, they could force a re-copy and it would work.

```
import socket
```

issue_title: Recurring task offset
issue_body:
On occasion there is a need for a recurring function, like reading a collection of points every _n_ seconds to not be exactly aligned with the _n_ boundary.  For example, reading something every minute is a 60-second recurring task but should be offset by 5 seconds so rather than reading at 01:15:00, 01:16:00, 01:17:00, ..., the readings are at 01:15:05, 01:16:05, 01:17:05, etc.

Add an optional *offset* parameter to `RecurringTask`, a positive value is "forward" in time and a negative value is "backward", so a value of -5000 would adjust the same example interval to 01:14:55, 01:15:55, 01:16:55, etc.  The value is in milliseconds to be consistent with the *interval* parameter.

There is no impact on existing applications.

issue_title: I Am Router To Network Helper Function
issue_body:
Create a NSAP function:

    i_am_router_to_network(self, adapter=None, destination=None, network=None)

If the adapter is not None, the destination must be a local station address, local broadcast address, a remote station address with a network number that matches the adapter, or a remote broadcast address that matches. If the adapter is None, the destination must be a remote station address or a remote broadcast address and the adapter will be select by the network number in the address, the downstream NPDU will have a magical local station address or local broadcast address. If both the adapter and destination are None, all of the adapters will get a local broadcast message with the created network list. If the network is not None, the message will be constructed as a list with one element. If the network is None, the list will be constructed as if as unconstrained WhoIsRouterToNetwork was received on the adapter.

Add a deferred call to this after setting up the stack on router samples.

issue_title: Customize ajax error message when DEBUG = False
issue_body:
Hi, 

When an error occurs in production, we get, by default, an alert with the message "An error occurred while processing an AJAX request."

I know we can override the onError javascript function to display a custom message, but it would be helpful to be able to customize the default message. The current message is very "developer" oriented message, but not very user friendly (many users don't even know what an AJAX request is).

Besides, my sites are in French and Italian and I would like them to have by default a message in their own language.

I see 2 ways to achieve the above:
1) add a ugettext_lazy to the shortcuts.py file where the message is hard-coded. This way, we can always translate the sentence above to something completely different (more user friendly) even in English.
2) add a variable in the settings and use it instead of the hard-coded message. The hard-coded message would be used in the case the variable is not set in the settings file. The user would put the ugettext/ugettext_lazy is his settings directly or not according to his own needs. In this cause, we could create a dictionnary DJANGO_AJAX and add all the settings related to this app into it (like django-rest-framework does). This avoids having multiple variables across the settings.

I prefer the option 2, but am obviously open to discussion.

I'm willing to implement it and make a pull request if you agree.
What do you think?
Thanks

issue_title: Add explainer to docs about installation issues
issue_body:
although #202 improved installation success with bare python installs, it does not totally solve the runtime dependency issue because setuptools falls back onto easy_install for the setup_requires and install_requires dependencies. This means that installations may still fail as easy_installs are a bit brittle for some transitive dependencies and ultimately inefficient for things like numpy as it does not take advantage of wheels either. Ultimately try to patch all of the contingencies will take too much effort, so it would just be better to tell users to install wheel, numpy, and urllib3[secure] in that order before trying to install on a clean python.  

see:
https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/917
https://pip.readthedocs.io/en/1.4.1/cookbook.html#controlling-setup-requires
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/

issue_title: Add easier way to do lists(..., unique_by=lambda x: x)
issue_body:
`unique_by` is easy to extend, which is nice, but it would also be nice to cover what is probably the most common case -- testing object uniqueness. In this case, we can currently do `lists(..., unique_by=lambda x: x)`, but that seems a little clumsy. It would be better to have a boolean flag that could control it.

Here's basically how I imagine it:

- if `unique` is False and `unique_by` is None, then nothing happens
- if `unique` is True and `unique_by` is None, then we make the items unique using item equality
- if `unique` is True or False and `unique_by` is not None, then we implicitly set `unique` to True, and make the items unique by calling `unique_by`

issue_title: Jupyter HTML repr hangs on large collections
issue_body: Due to [this bug/behavior in ipython](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/9771), `__repr__` and `_repr_html_` are called when a sequence is the last element evaluated in a jupyter notebook. Since `__repr__` prints a string of the entire collection so that `eval` can take it back in, this is extremely expensive while at the same time `_repr_html_` is written explicitly to give a summary view to avoid this problem.

issue_title: RFC: Update the test runner to pytest
issue_body:
@erikrose Nose is [no longer actively maintained](http://nose.readthedocs.io/en/latest/):
>Nose has been in maintenance mode for the past several years and will likely cease without a new person/team to take over maintainership. New projects should consider using Nose2, py.test, or just plain unittest/unittest2.

While it's essential "done" and is in little danger of breaking, py.test has added a  number of very nice features which make testing faster and much cleaner (e.g. descriptive errors for failures when using `assert foo(bar) == baz` styles). 

I've found myself missing pytest features when working on `parsimonious`, so if you're amenable to switching to pytest, I'd be happy to submit a PR. If not, please close this issue.

Thanks!

issue_title: Refactor HeartbeatMonitor
issue_body:
Current implementation has a method:
```python
    def heartbeat_received(self):
        if self.heartbeat_timeout_callback is not None:
            self.heartbeat_timeout_callback.cancel()
            self.monitor_heartbeat()
```
This is called for all events received from rabbitmq, which can be alot! If we go into asyncio's loop implementation we will see that a `cancel` for a handle will not remove it, but set a tombstone. Calling many `cancel` methods will flood the `self._scheduled` dequeue of the loop with canceled handles, resulting in poor performance. see [asyncio.base_events source](https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.5/Lib/asyncio/base_events.py#l1123)
Proposed enhancement:
Do not cancel the handle, rather just set the time of last frame in `_last_received`. The callback should check if _last_received was updated and if was reschedule itself to new time until no updates arrive.

issue_title: django_app/default: Migration 0003_alter_email_max_length wrong for Django 1.7
issue_body:
Hi,

Django 1.8 changed the default `max_length` for `EmailField` to 254. In response, python-social-auth added migration 0003_alter_email_max_length, which sets its own email field's length to 254. However, the email field itself does not have an explicit max_length specified, so on Django 1.7 (where the default `max_length` is still 75) Django issues this message (when running `migrate` and there are no other migrations to run):
```
  Your models have changes that are not yet reflected in a migration, and so won't be applied.
  Run 'manage.py makemigrations' to make new migrations, and then re-run 'manage.py migrate' to apply them.
```
Of course, if you follow that advice, you get an automatic migration.

Proper fix, probably, is setting the `Code.email`'s length to 75 explicitly as long as Django 1.7 is supported (254 is also an option, but may be a problem if the value is then copied into other, default-lengthed, email fields).

issue_title: coxph: different results vs R
issue_body:
It would be neat to see comparisons with R's coxph(). For example, R's coxph() produces quite different coefficient results on the rossi data set. **E.g. Lifeline's coefficient for age is over 6x larger than that of R's.**

Perhaps I've overlooked something simple? I haven't tried altering default params in Lifelines for fit() such as alpha or penalizer. I'd appreciate comments on this one! Details of results below.


Results from R:
Code:
> library(survival)
> fit <-coxph(Surv(dat$week, dat$arrest) ~ dat$fin + dat$age + dat$race + dat$wexp + dat$mar + dat$paro + dat$prio, data=dat)
> summary(fit)
Results:
>n= 432, number of events= 114 


 >var  coef   

>dat$fin -0.37942
>dat$age  -0.05744 
>dat$race  0.31390
>dat$wexp -0.14980
>dat$mar  -0.43370
>dat$paro -0.08487 
>dat$prio  0.09150 

Results from Lifelines, via:
http://lifelines.readthedocs.org/en/latest/Survival%20Regression.html#cox-s-proportional-hazard-model)

> n=432, number of events=114

> var coef

> fin  -1.897e-01
> age  -3.500e-01 
> race  1.032e-01  
> wexp -7.486e-02 
> mar  -1.421e-01 
> paro -4.134e-02 
> prio  2.620e-01


The coefficients look quite different to me. For example the age coefficients vary significantly.  R: coef(age) = -0.057 and coef(age)/coef(race) = -0.183 whereas in Lifelines, coef(age) = -0.344 and coef(age)/coef(race) = -3.378. Similar discrepancies for other vars such as mar.

On the other hand, some statistics seem to agree better such as 'z' (not displayed above).

issue_title: ldap3.Server, add get_info=ldap3.NONE ?

Hi, got everything working nicely with django-python3-ldap, syncing, auth, against Active Directory. Nice clean integration with Django, thanks for putting this together.

Would it be possible to set get_info=ldap3.NONE when creating the ldap3.Server in ldap.py, ~line 142:  

    try:
        with ldap3.Connection(ldap3.Server(
            settings.LDAP_AUTH_URL,
            allowed_referral_hosts=[("*", True)],
    +        get_info=ldap3.NONE),

```Python
class Post(db.Model):
```

issue_title: Need full example with forms
issue_body:
The sample_tree is not very complete (besides being way too sketchy).  No reply forms, for example.

Used to be a full working in example in an older version of Pinax, but that was for the older version of threadedcomments.

The only one I could find at http://code.google.com/p/django-threadedcomments/wiki/SitesUsingThreadedComments that is open source is Skyl's, which uses the older Pinax version.

And several other possibilities have switched to Disqus.  I don't want to; using threadedcomments on an intranet-type system, and need the comments linked to our own user profiles.

So I'm slogging away on my own implementation, which I will post  when it works.  But the threadedcomments app really needs one.  

If anybody knows of anything I missed, please let me know.

And also, what happened to the django-threadedcomments Google group?

Thanks,
Bob Haugen

issue_title: Custom data fixture for specific field on a model
issue_body:
I think it would be really useful to be able to define custom data fixtures for specific fields on a model. So for example, say I have a following model

```python
class Book(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=255)
    number_of_pages = models.IntegerField()
```

I would want to define special behavior for data generation for field `number_of_pages`, for example data fixture for `number_of_pages` could be random between `[10,100]`. It's different than what already exists in DDF in that it's specific to a model field vs a field in general (http://django-dynamic-fixture.readthedocs.org/en/latest/data_fixtures.html#custom-field-fixture).
It would be useful to be able to set something like this.

issue_title: pipchecker doesn't find misconfigured pypi packages
issue_body:
I've encountered problems with running pipchecker when there's a package which is misconfigured on pypi with underscores instead of dashes.  For example:

```
% manage.py pipchecker
django-celery-beat 1.0.1         release is not on pypi (check capitalization and/or --extra-index-url)
django-polymorphic 1.3           release is not on pypi (check capitalization and/or --extra-index-url)
```

Each of these packages seems to have been set up incorrectly to use underscores instead of dashes, and I've filed bugs for both of these packages to change their pypi entries (hopefully)
https://github.com/django-polymorphic/django-polymorphic/issues/319#issuecomment-345702344
https://github.com/celery/django-celery-beat/issues/85

But I also think it would be great if pipchecker could be more resilient.  I've tested a fix that seems to work which when searching the parsed pypi xml data searches for both the proper name, and a transformed name where dashes are replaced by underscores.

`available = pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name, True)`
->
`available = pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name, True) or  pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name.replace('-','_'), True)`

issue_title: Blocking on recv when using ioloop
issue_body:
I have a simple ZMQ socket that for doing a simple REQ-REP, and now I'd like to change it so that it runs on top of the ioloop. But I can't seem to find the right approach for using the callbacks with the ioloop.

I used to have this function:

    def ping(): 
        context = zmq.Context().instance()
        socket = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
        socket.connect("tcp://localhost:%s" % port)
        socket.send(amsg)
        result = socket.recv()
        socket.close()
        return result

Now I'm trying to write this using `ZMQStream` and the ioloop, but I'm not sure how to return the argument to `_recv` from `ping2()`:

    def ping2(): 
        context = zmq.Context().instance()
        socket = context.socket(zmq.REQ):
        socket.connect("tcp://localhost:%s" % port)
        stream = ZMQStream(socket)

        stream.send(amsg)
        def _recv(amsg):
           # Must return amsg from ping2?!
        result = stream.on_recv(_recv)
        stream.close()
        return result

Tornado has support for the `yield` syntax for giving control back to the ioloop, and then continuing from there (http://tornado.readthedocs.org/en/latest/gen.html):

        ...
        response = yield http_client.fetch("http://example.com")
        ...

Being able to write something like this would be neat:

        result = yield stream.recv()

Are there any other ways of writing `ping2()` that I am missing?

issue_title: pkg-config used to get prefix for libzmq, but should be used to get full paths
issue_body:
pkg-config is currently used in setup.py like:

    pkg-config --variable=prefix --print-errors libzmq

But pkg-config can be used to get all the information needed to build against libzmq, rather than building the cflags/ldflags from the prefix. Attempting to construct it by doing

    pjoin(prefix, 'lib')

Can just lead to sadness when building in an environment with non-standard library dirs (as I've been doing, on a system that places 64-bit libraries into $PREFIX/lib64). It would be better to just use *all* the information pkg-config can provide in this case. (I may be able to provide a patch, but I need to check on some employer policies first; it's also a fairly simple exercise to get it basically working)

issue_title: Inline Spark job fails when depedency reads a manually set configuration value
issue_body:
Using luigi 2.3.2 with python 2.7 and spark 2.0.1 on Hadoop 2.7.1.2.3.4.0-3485 (HDP)

First, I don't know if that is an intended behavior but I believe there is an issue here.

My  task fails because one of its dependencies reads one parameter from the configuration. Now, in our setup we load the configuration from non standard location (i.e. not from `client.cfg` or `luigi.cfg` in the working directory), furthermore we set configuration options after those files are loaded.

So it looks, like the run-time configuration is not ship with the job, as the Spark code  is executed on the cluster, that Python instance does not have that configuration loaded and thus it fails.

This issue does not  affect normal Luigi Hadoop JobTasks (as the inner workings are quite different).

## Symptom
I sent An description specific to my problem to the mailing list: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/luigi-user/0uCnIdnJd_Q

Below you will find a sample code for reproducing the issue. 

## Steps to reproduce

Create luigi_test.py containing:

```python
import luigi.contrib.spark
import luigi.contrib.hdfs
import luigi


class RequiresSomething(luigi.Task):
	a_parameter = luigi.Parameter(
		config_path={'section': 'custom', 'name': 'parameter'}
	)

	def output(self):
		return luigi.contrib.hdfs.HdfsTarget('test')

	def run(self,):
		o = self.output()
		with o.open('w') as f:
			f.write('test')


class DoNothingTask(luigi.contrib.spark.PySparkTask):

	def requires(self):
		return RequiresSomething()

	def main(self, sc):
		_input = self.input()


if __name__ == '__main__':
	config = luigi.configuration.LuigiConfigParser.instance()
	config.set('custom', 'parameter', '4')
	luigi.run(main_task_cls=DoNothingTask)


```

Run python2 luigi_test.py

## Workarounds and Ideas

Not that if the parameters are a standard configuration file like  'client.cfg' this error does not ocurr.

They way I found to workaround is via configuration add a  `client.cfg` path to the `files` option in the `spark` configuration. Another ways is to add those parameters to the calling class (`DoNothingTask`) and explicitly pass them down.  

Any idea where to look to solve this in a cleaner way? I am willing to work on this issue.

issue_title: Moving a LocalTarget does not change its attribute path
issue_body:
I have noticed that using `LocalTarget`'s `move` method, effectively changes its location on the local fs.
However, the `path` attribute of the `LocalTarget` object remains unchanged, thus, trying to remove it afterwards, by calling the `remove` method, does not work.

An example:
```python
# Instantiating an object
target = luigi.LocalTarget('/tmp/test.csv')
# Writing something to the file
with target.open('w') as fout:
    fout.write('asd')
# Renaming the file on the local fs
target.move('/tmp/test_2.csv')
# Raising an error, because the original file does not exist anymore:
target.remove()
```

The question is: Should the `path` of the object be updated after a `move` operation, thus "tracking" the object through the local fs?
If so, I'll be glad to submit a PR to solve this issue!

issue_title: download_fileobj with a file object in append mode can mess up file content
issue_body:
Environment
-----------------

Python 3.6.1
boto3 (1.4.5)

Why do I use it
--------------------

I am using the UNLOAD function from Redshift to dump data as a csv to S3.
Redshift maximizes performance by handling multiple chunks simultaneously and writes them on S3 separately.
As the final destination will be a local file on the user's hard drive, I use _download_fileobj()_ to stream each chunk into a single file.

The problem
------------------

boto3 handles this with a multipart download and often messes with the local file content.
This does __not__ happen when using a file object in write mode.
This does __not__ happen when disabling the multi part download with 

`
config = boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig(
    use_threads = False        
)
`

Related issues
---------------------

This issue seems to be  related to #1304 

Reproduce the issue
----------------------------

I created a mock dataset that I put on a public S3 bucket and created a code snippet to reproduce the issue.

```
import boto3
import csv

bucket           = 'rl-boto3-issues-mock-data' 
file_key         = 'mock_dataset.csv' 
output_file_path = 'output_file.csv'

s3     = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3.Bucket(bucket)

#Create file & write header
header = 'index1;index2;location;consumption\n'
with open(output_file_path, 'wt') as file_object:
    file_object.write(header)

#Dump binary in append mode
with open(output_file_path, 'ab') as file_object:

    bucket.download_fileobj(
        Key     = file_key, 
        Fileobj = file_object,
    )

#Read whole csv
with open(output_file_path, 'r') as file_object:

    header = next(file_object)

    reader = csv.reader(
        file_object,
        delimiter  = ';',
    )

    csv_data = list(reader)

#Check csv integrity
for index, row in enumerate(csv_data):
    
    nb_cells = len(row)

    if nb_cells != 4:
        print('Row {0} : number of cells is {1} and should be 4 !'.format(index, nb_cells))
        print('Row is : \n')
        print(row)
        print('\n')
        break
```

issue_title: CLI design principles and strategy
issue_body:
Replaces https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/issues/10.

We're developing a simple and easy to use CLI for the mapbox client module both as a service to customers and as a check on the usability of the module.

The CLI should be 99% about arg/opt definition and interoperation with other programs in the shell.

The CLI will have first class support for JSON and GeoJSON.

We'll rely on other programs like sed, awk, jq for finer parsing of responses from APIs at first, absorbing some of this when it makes sense.

issue_title: upload_docs: AttributeError with Sphinx 1.6
issue_body:
I'm not entirely positive if this should be filed as a Sphinx bug, or a setuptools bug...

Running `python setup.py build_sphinx upload_docs` with Sphinx 1.6 causes [upload_docs.py line 65](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/upload_docs.py#L65) to throw `AttributeError: builder_target_dir`. *(Tested with python 3.5.2, setuptools 35.0.2, sphinx 1.6.2)*

It looks like Sphinx 1.6 changed things to support multiple builders, and renamed the attribute to ``build_sphinx.builder_target_dirs`.  

I know upload_docs is deprecated for pypi, but it's still quite useful for staging using alternate package repos such as devpi.  

I'm happy to try and help, though I don't know the internals here very well; or refile this with Sphinx if it's something they should fix on their end.

issue_title: Make more use of setup.cfg
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Anonymous**

---

Right now setuptools uses setup.cfg only for configuration options of commands, e.g.

```
#!python

python setup.py build_ext --inplace 
```

is equal to 

```
#!python

[build_ext]
inplace=1
```

in setup.cfg. Why not allow users to define most of the configuration options to the _setup_ command inside setup.cfg? It seems there was some effort done in [disutils2](http://alexis.notmyidea.org/distutils2/setupcfg.html) to allow that. As the maintainer of [PyScaffold](pyscaffold.readthedocs.org), I implemented my own [logic](http://pyscaffold.readthedocs.org/en/v2.2/configuration.html) but have a strong feeling that setuptools should directly implement this. Having a purely declarative way of defining the settings for 95% of all use cases would help beginners and intermediate Python developers a lot. 

---
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/issue/394

issue_title: sparse run with named streams not compatible with Storm 1.0.1
issue_body:
Hello,

the currently stable streamparse 3.0.0 is not compatible with Storm 1.0.1, which is the latest official release available on storm.apache.org. It requires at least Storm 1.0.2 or 1.1.0, because `FluxShellSpout.setNamedStream` and `FluxShellBolt.setNamedStream` are [only available since then](https://github.com/apache/storm/blob/v1.0.2/external/flux/flux-wrappers/src/main/java/org/apache/storm/flux/wrappers/bolts/FluxShellBolt.java#L100).

Since 1.0.2 and 1.1.0 are only [released on GitHub](https://github.com/apache/storm/releases/tag/v1.0.2) and not yet available on maven this might be a serious problem. Streamparse also uses 1.0.1 as a default in the examples.

issue_title: Fix warnings in setuptools 8.0
issue_body:
As of setuptools 8.0, which introduced some new version parsing and full support for PEP 440, we get a couple warnings when running setup.py:

```
    /home/iguananaut/.virtualenvs/tmp-dea251bdca0911e7/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py:186: RuntimeWarning: You have iterated over the result of pkg_resources.parse_version. This is a legacy behavior which is inconsistent with the new version class introduced in setuptools 8.0. That class should be used directly instead of attempting to iterate over the result.
      stacklevel=1,
    /home/iguananaut/.virtualenvs/tmp-dea251bdca0911e7/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/setuptools/dist.py:284: UserWarning: The version specified requires normalization, consider using '1.1.dev0' instead of '1.1.dev'.
      self.metadata.version,
```

I'm not sure what the first one is aobout, I'm sure we can figure it out.  The second one is because we use "dev" version numbers like "1.1.dev".  But if I understand correctly (and I need to double check this) PEP-440 requires that dev versions have some unique specifier following it and can't just be bare.  We already generate a 'revision' number that we attach (like 1.1.dev1234) based on a count of git changesets.  But this count (unlike the githash) does not get stored in version.py for use in source dists of dev versions.  So that should be fixed as well.

issue_body:

```
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jsonschema/validators.py", line 122, in validate

jsonschema.exceptions.RefResolutionError: HTTPConnectionPool(host='127.0.0.1', port=80): Max retries exceeded with url: /schema/marc21/bibliographic/bd-v1.0.0.json (Caused by NewConnectionError('<requests.packages.urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x7fbd19946dd0>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno 111] Connection refused',))
```

The web container does not have access to the nginx container.

Ideally, the schemas can be checked locally, not needing the HTTP access. This is connected to `JSONSCHEMAS_HOST` settings that use 127.0.0.1 when running via docker:

```console
$ git grep SCHEMA scripts/
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_CONF=`cat <<EOF
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_ENDPOINT='/schema'
scripts/create-instance.sh:JSONSCHEMAS_HOST='http://${INVENIO_WEB_HOST}'
scripts/create-instance.sh:echo "${JSONSCHEMAS_CONF}" >> ../../var/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}-instance/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}.cfg
scripts/populate-instance.sh:SCHEMA="http://${INVENIO_WEB_HOST}/schema/marc21/bibliographic/bd-v1.0.0.json"
scripts/populate-instance.sh:demouuids=$(dojson -i $demomarc21pathname -l marcxml do marc21 schema ${SCHEMA} | \
```

(IIRC I tried to use `localhost` in the past but it wasn't recognised either.)

One option would be to try to supply real IP address, but the best would be to simply recognise addresses such as `0.0.0.0`, `127.0.0.1`, `localhost` as being local, and do the schema validation in Python, not having to access the schemas over the web at all.

P.S. Centralising the ticket here, but we can solve it in the appropriate repository, depending on the chosen method.

```
  item.__name__
...sqlalchemy/ext/declarative/clsregistry.py:120: SAWarning: This declarative base already contains a class with the same class name and module name as flask_sqlalchemy.PageVersion, and will be replaced in the string-lookup table.
  item.__name__
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|PageVersion|pages_page_version, primary key column 'pages_page_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'pages_page_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|PageListVersion|pages_pagelist_version, primary key column 'pages_pagelist_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'pages_pagelist_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
...sqlalchemy/orm/properties.py:194: SAWarning: On mapper Mapper|RecordMetadataVersion|records_metadata_version, primary key column 'records_metadata_version.transaction_id' is being combined with distinct primary key column 'records_metadata_version.transaction_id' in attribute 'transaction_id'.  Use explicit properties to give each column its own mapped attribute name.
  self.columns[0], self.key))
```

The warnings are issued when Invenio-DB is [initialized here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-db/blob/master/invenio_db/ext.py#L98).

What happens is:
1. Flask UI app is created and Invenio-DB is initialised first time (above mentioned initialisation step is not reached).
2. Flask REST app is created (and Invenio-DB is initialised a second time (now above initialisation step is reached).

Above is fully normal when running e.g. development server or UI/API on the same Python interpreter.

The reason for above initialisation step was because sometimes loading order would prevent the versioning tables from being created (see 336a7ee7143fca86a15adddeaa17676788ea1099 - this was quite a pain to debug).

cc @jacquerie 

Goal is to ensure that Invenio-App-ILS does not have unnecessary warnings when you start the development server.

issue_title: global: auto-registration is broken
issue_body:
When user is not found on the db, we automatically register them with `password=None`, as seen here: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-oauthclient/blob/master/invenio_oauthclient/utils.py#L92-L103

But Passlib does not allow `None` passwords, although it allows empty ones (`''`). To verify it, run:
```
>>> from passlib.hash import oracle10
>>> hash = oracle10.encrypt(None, user='admin')
>>> hash = oracle10.encrypt('', user='admin')
```

Therefore, if the user is not already registered, an internal server error will occur.

issue_title: Don't assume __STDC_VERSION__ is defined
issue_body:
See https://travis-ci.org/quixdb/squash/jobs/148061907#L1491

Note that this is coming from types.h, so it happens when compiling code *using* brotli, not necessarily in brotli itself.  Even if you don't care about `-Wundef` in brotli, IMHO it should be possible for people who just want to use brotli to use whatever warning flags they want.

Untested, but this should do the trick:

```diff
diff --git a/common/types.h b/common/types.h
index 4b050af..3508d74 100644
--- a/common/types.h
+++ b/common/types.h
@@ -25,7 +25,7 @@ typedef __int64 int64_t;
 #endif  /* defined(_MSC_VER) && (_MSC_VER < 1600) */
 
 #if (!defined(_MSC_VER) || (_MSC_VER >= 1800)) && \
-    (defined(__cplusplus) || __STDC_VERSION__ >= 199901L)
+    (defined(__cplusplus) || (defined(__STDC_VERSION__) && __STDC_VERSION__ >= 199901L))
 #include <stdbool.h>
 #define BROTLI_BOOL bool
 #define BROTLI_TRUE true
```

issue_title: Value used for EARTH_RADIUS is bogus
issue_body:
The figure that geopy/distance uses for EARTH_RADIUS, 6372.795 km, is
bogus.  geopy cites Wikipedia, and indeed several years ago Wikipedia
included this number (the "ellipsoidal quadratic mean radius").  However
it was contributed by an enthusiastic and early contributor to
Wikipedia, who was, unfortunately, not very mathematically
sophisticated.  The best quantity to use for an equivalent spherical
radius is the IUGG mean radius R_1 = (2*a + b) / 3 = 6371.009 km (for
WGS84) -- possibly you could round this to 6371 km.  This value
minimizes the RMS error between great-circle and geodesic distances.
For the painful deconstruction of the ellipsoidal quadratic mean radius,
see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Great-circle_distance/Archive_1

I want to recommend that config by default (i.e., for the default verbosity level) say a little more about what it's doing with galaxies drawn from COSMOSCatalogs.

In particular, I have had multiple issues (like #802) where it would have been really helpful if the default verbosity level would issue a single statement at the start of a given file saying (a) exactly what catalog is being used and (b) whether the galaxies are real or parametric.

For example, in #802, I had used a template with parametric galaxies, then had the .yaml file later modify to use real galaxies, except it quietly ignored that.  It took me ages before I realized it was not in fact using real galaxies.  I had to go all the way up to verbosity=3, and then notice that it was calling the galaxy generator for parametric galaxies.  If the default verbosity had a single statement at the start of making each large image saying what COSMOSCatalog was used, and how it's being used, I think that would make debugging simple without adding too much clutter to the outputs.

Thoughts?

issue_title: asizeof does not properly size numpy arrays
issue_body:
Although numpy is not a part of the python standard library, it is a part of many python developer's standard toolkits. Therefore it seems like it would be useful to properly size numpy arrays. Currently asizeof does not properly size numpy arrays. eg:

```
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from pympler import asizeof
>>> example = np.arange(1000)
>>> asizeof.asizeof(example)
80
```
Clearly, an array of 1000 values should take more than 80 bytes of memory.
Fortunately numpy arrays have an "nbytes" property that indicates their size, thus

```
>>> example.nbytes
8000
```
It may be possible to get an accurate idea of numpy array memory usage using this property.

I ran these commands in Python 3.4 with pympler 0.4.3

issue_title: Consider adding a `desc` or `descending` keyword argument to `Query.order_by`
issue_body:
From Google internal bug 65127895 (intentionally not providing a URL).

----

Currently the sort order should be specified as a string:

```python
query = cities_ref.order_by('name', direction='DESCENDING').limit(3)
```

This is error prone, since a user may make a typo while typing the
string out. The following would be much simpler:

```python
cities_ref.order_by('name', desc=True)
```

We (the Firestore team) don't have a strong opinion on this. Note that the Java SDK uses an Enum, the JS SDK uses the string and the iOS SDK uses a boolean. So feel free to do what you think is more idiomatic.

/cc @schmidt-sebastian

issue_title: Bundle behaviour change for directories in v2
issue_body:
The `v2` engine currently captures matched directories in `Snapshot`s, but doesn't actually propagate them through to `FilesetWithSpec`. One effect of this is the `jvm_app` `bundles` that explicitly refer to directories don't actually end up recursively including the directory content.

The `fileset` args here are matching directories, rather than files, and the semantics of `fileset` are ill defined. The following bundle fileset:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/')
```
...will not match any files in v2. But changing the glob to:
```
bundle(fileset='some/example/directory/**/*')
```
...will recursively match all files under the directory, similar to the v1 engine.

----

I think there are two changes to make here.

1. We should include all matched paths in the FilesetWithSpec in order to support the existing bundle case, and because it is completely reasonable for `tasks`/`Rules` to be able to capture directories.
2. We should add a deprecation warning for bundles for the case where a bundle matches _only_  directories. The idea is not to ban matching directories in the long term (that's totally reasonable)... instead, the goal would be to try to detect cases where people were expecting everything under a directory to be recursively included by a `fileset`, and instead ask them to change to an explicit recursive glob if that's what they wanted.

This address 981 SAINT ALBANS ST. N. #303 generates this error. Saint Albans is the street name. 


`Traceback (most recent call last):

    raise RepeatedLabelError(address_string, parse(address_string), label)

issue_title: Skipping layers during build
issue_body:
When building the ibm-was-base layer [0]
I noticed that one of the layers to be merged were missing.
ibm-was-base uses layer ibm-im [1] which in turn uses ibm-base [2]. ibm-im is missing.

This is what it is happening. We first fetch the ibm-im layer in a temp directory under deps/layer/ https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/fetchers.py#L134 . Then we rename that temp directory to the name of the layer https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/fetchers.py#L58 (ibm-im). The InterfaceFetcher will extract https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/fetchers.py#L99 the last part of the repo path, wihich based on the interfaces entry [3] will be trunk and will rename the deps/layer/ibm-im to 
deps/layer/trunk https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/fetchers.py#L110
The next layer is ibm-base whose path also ends with trunk. Therefore it will overwrite the ibm-im layer already in the deps/layer/trunk .

Should the layers and interfaces be declared in a way in the  http://interfaces.juju.solutions/ so that we do not overwrite them while fetching or should we handle this in the charm-tools?

[0] http://bazaar.launchpad.net/~ibmcharmers/charms/trusty/layer-ibm-was-base/trunk/view/head:/layer.yaml
[1] https://launchpad.net/~ibmcharmers/charms/trusty/layer-ibm-im/trunk
[2] https://code.launchpad.net/~ibmcharmers/layer-ibm-base/trunk
[3] http://interfaces.juju.solutions/layer/ibm-im/

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm 2.2.0-0ubuntu1~ubuntu16.04.1~ppa2
charm-tools 2.1.4
```

I am using: Ubuntu Xenial

issue_title: Proper way to list container's "bind"-type volumes, ie. bind mounts?
Bound volumes must be repeated
issue_body:
From the [less verbose example of mounting volumes](http://docker-py.readthedocs.org/en/latest/volumes/),

``` python
container_id = c.create_container(
    'busybox', 'ls', volumes=['/mnt/vol1', '/mnt/vol2'],
    host_config=docker.utils.create_host_config(binds=[
        '/home/user1/:/mnt/vol2',
        '/var/www:/mnt/vol1:ro',
    ])
)
```

we can see that `'/mnt/vol1'` and `'/mnt/vol2'` must be repeated. A preferred API would simply be

``` python
container_id = c.create_container(
    'busybox', 'ls',
    volumes=['/var/www:/mnt/vol1:ro', '/home/user1/:/mnt/vol2']
)
```

In this API, `volumes`
1. is actually a useful parameter when one needs to explicitly bind, without the verbosity of `host_config` and `binds`;
2. mirrors precisely the [the API to `docker run` for mounting volumes](https://docs.docker.com/userguide/dockervolumes/#mount-a-host-directory-as-a-data-volume), so that the user can re-apply the paradigm she has already learned; and
3. removes the need for redundantly specifying the same volume.

Additionally, `volumes` could support the more explicit dictionary argument, similar to `binds`:

``` python
container_id = c.create_container(
    'busybox', 'ls',
    volumes={
        '/home/user1/': {
            'bind': '/mnt/vol2',
            'mode': 'rw',
        },
        '/var/www': {
            'bind': '/mnt/vol1',
            'mode': 'ro',
        }
    }
)
```

issue_title: Allow for custom logger to be passed to ApplicationSession
issue_body:
ApplicationSession does not currently allow a custom logger to be passed to the logger instance that txaio uses. It would be nice to allow a custom logger to be passed based on the standard lib logging module.

``` python
class ApplicationSession(BaseSession):
    """
    WAMP endpoint session.
    """

    log = txaio.make_logger() or self.config.logger  # NOTE: Or something like this

    def __init__(self, config=None):
        """
        Constructor.
        """
        BaseSession.__init__(self)
        self.config = config or types.ComponentConfig(realm=u"default")
```

``` python
class SessionComponent(ApplicationSession):
    def __init__(self, config=None, hostname=None, logger=None):
        ApplicationSession.__init__(self, config, logger)
```

issue_title: Cryptobox serializer should be configurable
issue_body:
WAMP-cryptobox should allow for different serializers to be used when serializing the application payload that is to be end-to-end encrypted. See [here](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/cryptobox.py#L231) and [here](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/cryptobox.py#L203).

The hooks to allow use of a configurable serializer are there, but it is hard-coded to "json" currently.

We should support all serializers that we support at the transport level.

Another question is what default cryptobox serializer to use when none was specified. And also how to let the user configure the cryptobox serializer if it shouldn't use the default one.

issue_title: Expose payload transparency for user code
issue_body:
"payload transparency" is a feature of WAMP AP. Please see [here](https://github.com/wamp-proto/wamp-proto/issues/228). In particular the use cases described there.

This feature is implemented in AutobahnPython and Crossbar.io, but is currently NOT exposed at the user API level in any direct way.

It is only used _internally_ to implement WAMP-cryptobox. And the latter feature is only experimental/alpha. So we can change things.

As from recent discussion on IRC (#autobahn), there is strong interest in letting user code hook into the "payload transparency" feature.

FWIW, and for the record, I was long fighting for NOT exposing this to user code. But I changed my mind;) Well, the accumulated feedback and arguments from multiple users finally convinced me that the required changes, introduction of new API and deviation from a WAMP principle (automatic payload conversion / serializer independence) are worth it. And that users should be free choose their tradeoff on their requirements - not us. We can't make the tradeoffs involved magically disappear, but I now think we can expose it in a way that still fits into WAMP and keeps the coherence intact.

issue_title: Refactor Iterators to use less inheritance
better "serialization directory exists" workaround for beaker
issue_body:
`allennlp train` crashes if the serialization directory already exists.

this is a problem on beaker, where the output directory necessarily already exists.

right now we have a workaround where if the serialization dir is `/output` we skip the check and proceed. this is fragile and non-obvious for our users.

I have talked to the beaker team about potentially having an environment variable that indicates that code is running on beaker. 

In the meantime (and/or instead), Christian (who is the one who ran into this issue) suggested that we instead change the logic to allow the serialization directory to exist *as long as it's empty*. This seems pretty reasonable to me and makes the code less fragile.

issue_body:
Nitzan Ravhon reports:

from music21 import *

m = stream.Measure()
m.timeSignature = meter.TimeSignature('4/4')
for i in range(4):
    m.append(note.Rest('c4', quarterLength = 0.25))
    m.repeatAppend(note.Note('c4', quarterLength = 0.25), 3)
m2 = m.makeBeams()

beams are wrong for the first sixteenth after each rest -- TimeSignature.getBeams() does not take into account whether the duration is beamable or not.  a problem.

issue_title: xlsx recognised as zip
Interface / syntax to use Python 2 or customized interpreter
issue_body:
Suggested by @jean997 One obvious place to add such a configuration is: 

```
simulate: Python(res = a_python_2_function())
  $data: res
  @CONF: python_version=2
```

A more general solution is:

```
simulate: Python(res = a_python_2_function())
  $data: res
  @CONF: interpreter = "python2"
```

Then this will allow people to do something like:

```
simulate: R(res = 1)
  $data: res
  @CONF: interpreter = "/path/to/specific/version/of/Rscript"
`````

Or something like, 

```
simulate: R(res = 1)
  $data: res
  @CONF: interpreter = "R CMD BATCH --whatever-options"
```

<!--
Hi!

This is an issue tracker for conda -- the package manager.  File feature requests
for conda here, as well as bug reports about something conda has messed up.

If your issue is a bug report or feature request for:
  * a specific conda package from Anaconda ('defaults' channel):
      ==> file at https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues
  * a specific conda package from conda-forge:
      ==> file at the corresponding feedstock under https://github.com/conda-forge
  * repo.continuum.io access and service:
      ==> file at https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues
  * anaconda.org access and service:
      ==> file at https://anaconda.org/contact/report
  * commands under 'conda build':
      ==> file at https://github.com/conda/conda-build
  * commands under 'conda env':
      ==> please file it here!
  * all other conda commands that start with 'conda':
      ==> please file it here!

-->

**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

In a csh shell, after sourcing the '~/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.csh'

When activating the environment the original prompt is not a part of the new prompt.

Neither is it reverted to the original prompt when deactivating.

Additionally, when deactivating the 'CONDA' specific env vars are not removed from the environment.



### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- If the current behavior is a bug, please provide specific, minimal steps to independently reproduce.
     Include the exact conda commands that reproduce the issue and their output between the ticks below. -->
```
$> echo "'$prompt'"
'$> '
$> conda activate test_env
(test_env) echo "'$prompt'"
'(test_env) '
(test_env) conda deactivate
env | grep CONDA
_CONDA_EXE=/home/jrevans/miniconda3/bin/conda
_CONDA_ROOT=/home/jrevans/miniconda3
CONDA_SHLVL=0
CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV=test_env
CONDA_PREFIX=/home/jrevans/miniconda3/envs/test_env
CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER=(test_env) 
CONDA_PYTHON_EXE=/home/jrevans/miniconda3/bin/python

```


## Expected Behavior
<!-- What do you think should happen? -->

The prompt needs to be preserved throughout and the conda env vars need to be removed from the environment.

I have attached a version of the '.../etc/profile.d/conda.csh' script that fixes the prompt problem.

The env var problem can be fixed by changing the results of 'conda shell.csh deactivate' to use 'unsetenv' instead of 'unset'

[conda.csh.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/1748844/conda.csh.txt)

issue_title: How to avoid superceding?
issue_body:
I was trying to install a Conda package during a demo.  I was surprised to see that the Conda package plan decided to use a Conda package I created end of 2015 and that is available in my channel.  I understand why matching packages from explicitly listed channels might  have preference, even if it results in a downgrade.  But is there a way to say "don't do that?" I imagine if I had a "newer" version in an explicitly listed channel then I might expect different behavior than an older version, but maybe not.

And in the case of Conda (unsurprisingly) there are clearly dangers to having your own conda package in a channel.  It isn't a dependency of the listed `genepattern` package, so this is just the *"automatically update conda and conda-env"* behavior that is then pulling in my custom Conda build. issue_title: conda env update --prune does not remove installed packages not defined in environment.yml
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior `conda env update --prune` does not remove packages no longer referenced in the `environment.yml` file. ### Steps to Reproduce Define an `environment.yml` file in a project directory as follows: name: new-project-env dependencies: - python=3 - numpy In the project directory run the following commands: conda env create conda activate new-project-env python import numpy exit() This succeeds as expected. Update the `environment.yml` file as follows, **removing numpy**, name: new-project-env dependencies: - python=3 Run the following commands in the project directory: conda deactivate conda env update --prune conda activate new-project-env python import numpy exit() **This numpy import succeeds and seems like a bug**. ## Expected Behavior Per [the docs](https://conda.io/docs/commands/env/conda-env-update.html) (--prune means "remove installed packages not defined in environment.yml") I expect that after installing a package (numpy in the example above) and subsquently removing it from `environment.yml` the package should be removed from the relevant conda environment. ## Current Workaround I've found that we can use `conda env create --force` to overwrite the existing environment and thus ensure that the environment accurately reflects the `environment.yml` file. But this requires recreating the environment from scratch. ## Environment Information

issue_title: Conda wrapper script gets unusable if path to python interpreter gets too long
issue_body:
We first encountered the problem using Jenkins CI for automated testing and deployment of our projects. We were able to generalize this issue as it is a problem with the conda wrapper script.

Bug Description:
If the full path to the python interpreter installed in a conda environment exceeds 126 characters in Linux, the conda wrapper script gets unusable.

This effect is due to the she-bang line in the conda wrapper script exceeding the setting of BINPRM_BUF_SIZE, a Linux kernel setting found in include/linux/binfmts.h.

Expectation: While this problem is not a bug in Anaconda itself, the conda wrapper script still should detect this issue and work around it. Possible workarounds can be found here:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10813538/shebang-line-limit-in-bash-and-linux-kernel

issue_title: Closing WebSocket prior to Ready event
issue_body:
I think I've run into a bug or two with 0.2.4 when calling `WebSocket.close()` prior to a `Ready` event.  Consider the following example code

```python
import logging

from lomond import WebSocket, events

logging.basicConfig(format='%(message)s', level=logging.DEBUG)
ws = WebSocket('ws://echo.websocket.org')

for event in ws:
    logging.info(event)
    if isinstance(event, events.Connecting):
        logging.info('Closing @Connecting...')
        ws.close()
    if isinstance(event, events.Connected):
        logging.info('Closing @Connected...')
        ws.close()
```

which produces
```
Connecting(url='ws://echo.websocket.org')
Closing @Connecting...
WebSocket unavailable; data not sent
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 12, in <module>
    ws.close()
  File "lomond\websocket.py", line 234, in close
    self.state.sent_close_time = self.session.session_time
  File "lomond\session.py", line 60, in session_time
    return time.time() - self._start_time
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for -: 'float' and 'NoneType'
```

The exception from the Connecting stage can be remedied by checking the return value from `_send_close()` when called in `WebSocket.close()` and conditionally not setting the state when the return value is `False`.  This will get you further along to the Connected stage, where the same exception will occur.  The solution here is less clear.  The root problem is due to session time not being available until a `Ready` event is handled at which point `WebSocketSession._start_time` is set.

My use case has the event loop running in a thread and I'd like to be able to exit the event loop gracefully (and subsequently exit the thread) by calling `WebSocket.close()` from the main thread.  But, in some corner cases I try to tear things down during either the Connecting or Connected stage.  Even assuming a fix to the issues above, there is no actual connection during the Connecting stage so there is nothing to close.  Is there a recommendation for a way to gracefully exit the event loop in a situation like this?  My only idea right now if to repetitively call `close()`in a loop until `WebSocket.sent_close_time` is not `None`.

issue_title: reads with samflags 99/147 have reference coordinates differing by one compared to 88/163
issue_body:
Hi all,

I am using pysam to parse paired-end whole-genome bisulfute sequencing bam files generated by Bismark/Bowtie2. 

I am using pysam to pull out all reads in specific regions. For reads with samflags 88 or 163 everything appears to be as expected. For example: If i use `read.get_reference_positions()` and compare the bases at these positions back reference back to the genome I find the base I expect, in my case  the CpG I am looking for. 

However if the read has samflags of 99 or 147 the base reference position appears to be shifted by one compard to its expected reference position in the genome. For example, when the reference position should point to a CpG it actually points to an A  in a ACG or C in a CCG or even a G in a GCG. No matter the context they appears to be off by one base-pair.

Is this a bug? Or is there some underlying function I am just misunderstanding that has something to do with these samflags?

Thanks for your time.

Starting with OpenCobolIDE 4.5.1 the icon and .desktop files are being installed inside /usr/lib/python-3.4/site-packages when installing using pip3 on Fedora 22 Linux. For OpenCobolIDE 4.5.0 and earlier the paths to the .desktop file and icon were placed in /usr/share/applications and /usr/share/pixmaps now they are /usr/lib/python-3.4/site-packages/usr/share/applications and pixmaps. Because of this, there is no entry for OpenCobolIDE in GNOME's list of applications.

## System information

- Operating System: Linux (Fedora 22 Twenty Two)
- OpenCobolIDE: 4.6.0
- GnuCOBOL: 1.1.0
- Python: 3.4.2 (64bits)
- Qt: 5.4.1
- PyQt: 5.4.1
- pyqode.core: 2.6.3
- pyqode.cobol: 2.6.1
- pyqode.qt: 2.6.0
- pygments: 2.0.2
- QDarkStyle: 2.0

issue_title: Improve azure-keyvault custom Authentication mecanism
issue_body:
`requests` has nice ability to accept a subclass of AuthBase to custom the authentication process. This is callback-based, and `requests` gives to the `__call__` method a prepared query. Note that when you do `requests.get` or any methods, requests creates for you a prepared query first, and this is this query that is sent to `AuthBase` subclasses.
However, not all possible parameters are inside a prepared query. For example, `verify`, that you can specify in your call (ie: `requests.get(...., verify='myfile')`) is not used in the prepared query. In addition, we don't have access to the context (i.e. are we using a Session?).

KeyVault has a special behavior that requires to do a requests from AuthBase to get the token. With the preceding limitation, this implies that we don't have access to the following parameters from within an AuthBase: proxies, stream, verify, cert, timeout, alow_redirects. We don't have access too to the configuration of the Session that is doing the call. To end, AuthBase is called before environment variable like REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE or CURL_CA_BUNDLE are read.

The probable best approach is to provide auth dynamically:
```python
client.certificates.get(......., auth=KeyAuthBase(.....))
```
This way we can create this class with the context and drop it after the call. Another way is to add support for that in msrest. Since msrest owns the Session and the `requests` call, we are in the right place to fix the context.

FYI @schaabs

issue_title: Integrate new "az ad sp create-for-rbac --sdk-auth" command as SDK credentials
issue_body:
Coming next CLI release, we have an official format for credentials, created by the CLI. This issue is to track the support of this feature in the SDK, to achieve this kind of scenario:

```bash
az ad sp create-for-rbac --sdk-auth > ~/myazcredentials.json"
```
and then
```python
from azure.common.client_factory import get_client_from_auth_file
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
client = get_client_from_auth_file(ComputeManagementClient, "~/myazcredentials.json")
```

issue_title: create_random_name might not work well
issue_body:
As it stands, the two consumers of `azure-devtools` both override `ReplayableTest.create_random_name`, which is good because I think the default implementation might not work.

In particular, due to this line in `azure_devtools.scenario_tests.utilities.create_random_name` (which `IntegrationTestBase.create_random_name` calls):
``` python
random_bytes = os.urandom(int(math.ceil(float(padding_size) / 8) * 5))
```

the names it generates will be actually random and not consistent between test runs. (I haven't yet tested this but I can't see how it would be otherwise. This would also be a prime candidate for #5 as well.)

issue_title: Support global git hooks in git v2.9
issue_body:
From the [release notes](https://github.com/blog/2188-git-2-9-has-been-released):

>You can now specify a custom path for hooks. Git calls hook scripts to allow you to implement custom policy or actions when certain events occur. These hook scripts are found inside the .git directory of each repository, making them a pain to manage if you have a standard set of hooks for all of your repositories. Now you can store the hooks in one place and point all of the repositories at them with the new core.hooksPath config.

I'm thinking global config with repo local overrides, which solves the pain of manual syncing and updating hooks for every individual repo.

issue_title: Proposal for a simplified data store API
Run http server when event loop is already running.
issue_body:
I am trying to use aiohttp to run an http server as part of a larger app that already manages the event loop.  If I run `web.run_app()` I get an error because its trying to run the loop itself.  As the http server in my case is just a small and **optional** feature, it doesn't make sense for me to allow aiohttp to manage it.

Is there a way to acomplish this, aside from duplicating all of the work that `web.run_app()` is doing in my own app?  That is what I have done for now as a workaround, but its obviously not ideal as my app shouldn't know about those kinds of aiohttp interals. (Including making a call to the protected `web._make_server_creators` function and having to watch for any changes to `web.run_app()` across versions.

Ideally, aiohttp could just restructure things as to return 2 coroutines to be run on the loop (one for startup, one for shutdown), along with a helper function that does it for you which would keep compatibility with the current `web.run_app()` behavior.

issue_title: Recommended method of installation
issue_body:
The jupyter website recommends to install anaconda in order to get jupyter up and running.

I did a clean install of anaconda (for python 3.4) with the latest sh script of their website, added the bin folder to my Path and executed:

    conda update conda
    conda update anaconda
    conda update python
    conda install jupyter
    conda update jupyter

now jupyter is available in my shell, I can start both notebooks in the browser and as qtconsoles.

But there is a problem: It is impossible to import pyplot from matplotlib. Here is the pastebinhttp://pastebin.com/RJ0Mj2F0

I tried to install pygobject with pip, but apparently anaconda has its own copy of the python interpreter. Pygobject or pygtk are not in the anaconda channels.

This command:
    anaconda search -t conda pygtk

delivers:
    
    Using binstar api site https://api.anaconda.org
    Run 'anaconda show <USER/PACKAGE>' to get more details:
    Packages:
         Name                      |  Version | Package Types   | Platforms      
         ------------------------- |   ------ | --------------- | ---------------
         auto/pygtk-textbuffer-with-undo | 0.1.post2.dev74151128 | conda           | linux-64       
                                              : http://github.com/cfobel/pygtk_textbuffer_with_undo.git
         auto/pygtkhelpers         |    0.4.3 | conda           | linux-64       
                                              : http://packages.python.org/pygtkhelpers
         mike/pygtk-aio            | 2.24.32.win32.py2.7 | conda           | win-32         
                                              : PyGtk All In One
         ska/pygtk                 |   2.16.0 | conda           | linux-64       
                                              : Pipeline running tools
         vgauthier/pygtk           |   2.24.0 | conda           | osx-64         
                                              : PYGTK
    Found 5 packages

It seems like only ska/pygtk is supported on linux, but that is an outdated version of pygtk. The current version is 2.24.

What is the correct way to install jupyter ?
It would be great if you could add more information to your website. It seems like installation is straightforward, but without pyplot this is hardly usable.

issue_title: Spec and implementation of markdown extensions
issue_body:
There are many issues related to extending markdown to support different elements and use cases:

* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1292
* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/2300
* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1918
* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1095
* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/189
* https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/77
* https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/2958

And an ongoing discussion about switching to Commonmark: Related to https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1371

Let's bring these separate discussions together here and figure out a good way enable this in classic notebook, JupyterLab, nteract, nbconvert, nbviewer, etc. 

cc @ellisonbg @Carreau @takluyver @rgbkrk @minrk @blink1073 @jasongrout

issue_title: Remove numexpr dependency
issue_body: Proposed to remove dependency on numexpr as performance added is not that great and getting issues with CPU compatibility when running on heterogeneous cluster.

issue_title: Option to include all directories when downloading specific files
issue_body:
Feature Request:
> When I download specified files, it creates a main directory containing the project (with the project name) and then just the necessary subdirectories to fill in the requested file.  Would it be possible, or is there already a way, in which I could request specific files, but it would create the entire subdirectory tree of the project, just creating empty folders when none of the files from that folder are being selected for download?

There is currently no mechanism to download all directories when using the download command with the `--include <path>` argument.

If we were to add an option to include all directories(folders) we would need to add the check to PathFilteredProject:
https://github.com/Duke-GCB/DukeDSClient/blob/f4c192dfd6657a8d6f85ccb3050d4c9c936bfa25/ddsc/core/pathfilter.py#L161-L167

Need to figure out if this flag should/could have any effect when used with the exclude option.

issue_title: float() argument must be a string or a number, not 'FloatNode'
issue_body:
Since there is no support for numpy.linspace, I was trying to make a following workaround:
```
 def linspace(start, end, num):
     l = autograd.numpy.zeros((num))
     for i in range(num):
         c = i / (num-1)
         v = start * (1-c) + (c) * end
         l[i] = v
     return l
```
This is embedded in an algorithm where start and end depend on the parameters wrt. which we differentiate and num is a fixed constant. Unfortunately, this code produces an error:

`float() argument must be a string or a number, not 'FloatNode'''

in the line ` l[i] = v'.

I may by making a simple mistake here - if someone has a quick fix for my use case, I'd be very grateful.

issue_title: Permission with multiple TS acounts
issue_body:
User names in `.shed.yml` and the `.planemo.yml` should be checked before uploading. Repositories specifying a user in `.shed.yml` should be only created under this username.

Currently, it's possible to submit to a TS with just an API key, the user name is ignored. People with multiple TS accounts easily submit to the wrong group, sorry for that ;).

Related: https://github.com/galaxyproject/planemo/pull/224

issue_title: Finding Visual C++ compiler for Python with CMake (on appveyor)
issue_body:
Greetings, this is a copy of a message I posted to the cmake mailing list, but got no love there.  Since you all are closer to this topic, I hope you might know something helpful.

In compiling TBB, a conda-forge contributor ran across issues with CMake not finding the VS compiler for VS 2008 Win64: https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/533

The exact error is at https://ci.appveyor.com/project/conda-forge/staged-recipes/build/1.0.2118/job/t2axdaotmty35j6k#L216

Appveyor is an interesting platform, in that they install VS 2008 Express, then the VS compiler for Python (which adds the 64-bit compilation support).

I have been able to fix this in conda-build's test suite by adding some extra registry information and copying some files: https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/master/appveyor.yml#L71-L74

Is our version of cmake too old?  Is there some other way we can teach CMake to look in the right place, so that that ugly hack of editing the registry is not necessary?

issue_title: Get rid of requests dependency
issue_body:
Per a conversation with Coffee in #matrix-dev:matrix.org, requests is a rather heavyweight dependency when compared to the size of this library. Since we only use requests in the `_send` api method, using urllib instead of requests shouldn't add much extra burden to maintaining the sdk.

The biggest issue to overcome is rewriting all of the tests that use responses.

See also https://github.com/Matrixcoffee/urllib-requests-adapter
